title: Choosing the Right Archetype
concept: Matching the agent's structural pattern to the complexity of the target environment and task constraints.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what makes a learning agent special?

options.text: It never changes and repeats the same steps forever
It improves its behavior using feedback about past successes and failures
It only works if a human types every command

exercises.explain: A learning agent watches how its past runs went, keeps a critique of what worked or failed, and updates its behavior next time. That self-improvement loop is the whole point.

cards.title: One tool for every job?
cards.body: You wouldn't use a sledgehammer to hang a photo. The same is true for agents: a fancy learning agent is overkill for a task a simple rule can solve.

cards.title: Choosing the right archetype
cards.body: This means matching the agent's design pattern to how complex the environment is and what limits the task has. Simple, predictable tasks need simple agents; messy, changing ones need smarter agents.

cards.title: Picking a vehicle
cards.body: A skateboard is perfect for a smooth sidewalk, but useless on a mountain trail where you need a rugged jeep. You pick the vehicle that fits the terrain, just like you pick the agent that fits the task.

cards.title: Two tasks, two agents
cards.body: A thermostat turning on heat below 68°F just needs a simple reflex agent. A robot exploring an unknown building needs a model-based or learning agent to handle surprises.

cards.title: Myth: smarter is always better
cards.body: A more advanced agent isn't automatically the right choice. Extra complexity means slower, costlier, and harder to debug, so a simple agent often wins when the task is simple.

cards.title: Match the pattern to the task
cards.body: The environment's complexity should guide your pick, not the coolness of the design.

left.text: Predictable, fixed rules → reflex agent fits great

right.text: Changing, uncertain world → goal, model-based, or learning agent

exercises.prompt: A vending machine gives snacks based on the exact button pressed. Which archetype fits best?

options.text: A simple reflex agent that reacts to the current input
A learning agent that critiques its past runs
A model-based agent that maps the whole building
A goal-based agent that plans many steps ahead

exercises.explain: The task is fully predictable: one button, one snack. A simple reflex agent that reacts to current input is the right, low-cost fit. Anything fancier is wasted effort.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A more complex agent archetype is always the safer, better choice.
exercises.explain: False. Extra complexity adds cost, slowness, and bugs. For simple, predictable tasks, a simpler agent is the smarter pick.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about matching agents to tasks.
exercises.sentence: You should match the agent's design to the complexity of its environment.
exercises.explain: The key idea is matching the design to the environment's complexity: simple worlds get simple agents, messy worlds get smarter ones.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence about choosing an archetype was written by an AI?

options.text: Selecting an agent architecture requires weighing environmental complexity against task constraints to ensure an optimal fit.
Just pick the fancy one, it's cooler
my thermostat is broke again ugh

exercises.explain: The first is polished, formal, and evenly structured, typical AI phrasing. The others sound casual and human. Matching design to complexity and constraints is exactly the real lesson, though.

exercises.prompt: A delivery drone must fly through a busy, changing city with unpredictable weather and obstacles, and it should get better with each trip. Which archetype and reasoning fit best?

options.text: A simple reflex agent, because it reacts fastest to buttons
A learning agent, because the environment is complex and changing and the drone must improve over time
A reflex agent, because the city is fully predictable
Any agent works equally well since complexity doesn't matter

exercises.explain: The city is complex, uncertain, and always changing, and the goal explicitly requires improving over time. That calls for a learning agent, which handles surprises and updates its behavior from past trips. The big takeaway of this unit: don't pick the flashiest agent, pick the one whose structure matches the task's complexity and constraints.
